prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response:
There is perhaps no ethical dilemma more complex than the question of whether it is ever justified to deceive someone you love. On one hand, love implies a foundation of trust and honesty - qualities that lies inherently undermine. To deceive a beloved is to introduce a toxin into the relationship, a falsehood that can fester and erode the sacred bond between you.

And yet, is radical honesty always the highest moral good? There are times in life when the unvarnished truth can be cruel, wounding, unnecessarily painful. If a white lie can spare a loved one needless suffering, is it not sometimes the lesser evil? We lie to those we cherish when we tell a child that their beloved pet goldfish has gone to live on a farm upstate. We prevaricate when we assure our mother that her new haircut looks fabulous. We obscure when we insist to our despondent partner that they don't look fat in those pants. In such cases, perhaps a small deception serves a greater truth - the truth of our underlying love and compassion.

The key, it seems, is that lies are only permissible insofar as they are a unadorned reflection of a deeper loyalty - and that we never lose sight of the fundamental truth of our loving bond. We must be ever vigilant that our deceptions do not compound or metastasize, that our well-intentioned fabrications do not poison the essential honesty at the heart of love. Ultimately, while the occasional white lie may be an expression of care and protection, authentic love cannot long survive the acid drip of deceit. We owe those we love nothing less than truth.
